data.title: Nationwide GQG U.S. Quality Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

During the third quarter, the S&P 500 index increased 8.12% with 10 of the 11 sectors exhibiting a positive return. The largest drivers of the benchmark’s gain were information technology (+13.2%, contributing 435 basis points (BPs) to the performance of the S&P 500 index), communication services (+12.0%, +120 bps), and consumer discretionary (+9.5%, +100 bps).

Stylistically, the S&P 500 Growth Index (9.8%) easily outperformed the S&P 500 Value index (6.2%) during the period.

In the US, we believe the positive return in the equity market was driven by three catalysts. First, it was another solid earnings season for the S&P 500 with aggregate earnings per share (EPS) growth in 2Q25, reported during 3Q25, of 12% versus the prior year.

Second, investors continued to express optimism about a higher and longer capital expenditure cycle to support data center construction to satisfy expected demand for generative artificial intelligence applications. During 3Q25, one of the large language model developers signed a massive multi-year contract for computing power with a cloud service provider. If both parties can perform on that agreement, that would be a tailwind for the semiconductor and electrification supply chains.

Third, a weakening job market improved the likelihood that the Federal Reserve would loosen monetary policy. Non-farm payroll growth in August was less than expected while the unemployment rate ticked up from 4.2% to 4.3%. In addition, for the 12 months ending March 2025, the number of jobs estimated by the Bureau of Labor Statistics was revised down by more than 900,000. That was the largest preliminary revision on record going back to 2000. In September, the Fed indeed lowered its overnight policy rate target by 25 bps to 4.00-4.25%, the central bank’s first rate reduction in nine months. The Fed chairman referred to the new policy target as modestly restrictive, implying that additional rate cuts are possible this year if inflation does not spike and the recent softness in the labor market continues.

Regarding trade policy, the Trump administration appears to be following a template of 10% baseline tariffs on countries with which the US has a trade surplus, 15% on those with which the US has a small deficit (including Japan and the EU), and higher tariffs on countries with which the US has a large trade deficit. However, the trade agreements announced to date are not concrete deals but frameworks. Critical details on the trade policies have yet to be resolved, in our view, as the US tries to limit the re-routing of Chinese exports through other countries, among other goals. Importantly, the US Supreme Court is expected to rule within a few months on the legality of the administration’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act for certain tariffs.

The US 10-year Treasury yield entered the quarter at 4.23%, peaked in mid-July at 4.49%, and ended the period at 4.15%. Investment grade credit spreads in the US narrowed by ten bps in 3Q25 to 76 bps, while high-yield spreads tightened by 16 bps to 280 bps. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index (largely US Treasuries, investment grade corporates, and agency mortgage-backed securities) had a gain of 1.1% during the quarter. The US Dollar Index (DXY) strengthened 0.9% in the period.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index increased 2.6% during 3Q25 as gains in gold (+16.8%) and iron ore (+11.5%) were only partially offset by losses in wheat (-12.0%) and copper (-4.5%). Gold appears to be attractive as a safe-haven asset relative to the US dollar due to the country’s unpredictable trade policies, record debt levels, and the perception of waning central bank independence.

Sources: Bloomberg, CME Group, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, S&P Global, and WSJ.com

Portfolio Positioning

The utilities sector is the largest overweight in the portfolio with information technology the largest underweight. Headwinds include the potential unavailability of material and labor due to evolving trade and immigration policies from the Trump administration as the utility companies seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity, in our opinion. In addition, the sector faces regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind we believe is a continued increase in AI- related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

We reduced our exposure to the information technology sector. We are significantly underweight tech as we believe many tech names are overearning due to the acceleration of AI-related capital expenditures from the providers of cloud services as they construct data centers to meet expected demand from the developers of large language models (LLMs). Regarding the sustainability of the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending, we are skeptical. The proliferation of data center construction is likely to result in meaningful overcapacity of computing power, in our opinion, leading to declining cloud service pricing and poor economic returns. Also, we believe the LLMs are unlikely to generate the requisite subscription revenue from their user bases to pay for the computing power demand the LLMs are promising to the cloud service providers.

Finally, we think valuations still matter. Many tech stocks are expensive, in our view, with a risk of a quick, severe multiple compression if these companies lower their earnings guidance.

In contrast, we added exposure to certain names in the energy and consumer staples sectors as we found those opportunities more compelling on a risk-adjusted basis.

We do not target any specific factor exposures in the portfolio. We believe that backward-looking quantitative measures can give investors a false sense of diversification and precision. We prefer to focus on the potential of a permanent loss of capital by analyzing the end consumers of the businesses in which we invest and their behavior in various economic environments.

Outlook

We are overweight the Utilities sector. While the near-term supply of power in the US is expected to be static, we believe there are three catalysts driving an increase in electricity demand. First, the increasing use of generative AI applications is likely to require meaningful levels of computing power from data centers currently in operation, as well as those planned to open during the next several years. These data centers are expected to require additional power supplies. Second, certain areas of the country are benefiting from a migration in population. Third, we expect an increase in the onshoring of manufacturing plants in response to new trade policies in the US. As a result, we believe there is good visibility on annualized earnings growth for select utility names during the next five years.

We are also overweight Consumer Staples as we believe the fundamentals for certain tobacco businesses are solid with growth coming from next generation products. In addition, select tobacco stocks have what we consider to be attractive dividend yields. We are constructive on opportunities in the Financials sector as well. Certain property and casualty insurers have high recurring revenue, in our opinion, and the potential to mine their large data sets with AI applications to price risk more efficiently going forward.

We are underweight the Information Technology sector. We are skeptical that the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending is sustainable and believe certain tech names are overearning. In addition, many stocks in the sector are expensive, in our opinion, with a risk of compression in their valuation multiples.

Headwinds include the potential unavailability of material and labor due to evolving trade and immigration policies from the Trump administration as the utility companies seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity. In addition, the sector faces regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind is a continued increase in AI-related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance.

While we are not economists, we do monitor macro data as part of our risk framework. We underwrite the existing, and potential, companies in our portfolios focusing on the visibility of their future free cash flows. Ignoring the macro conditions in which these companies operate would impact the accuracy of any investor’s earnings outlook, in our opinion. However, adapting our portfolios to reflect new information is a hallmark of our process and we will continue to go where the data leads us.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC: Big Discount And High Yield Isn't The Bargain You Think
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to investing, many of us are drawn to higher yields. Especially if you're an income-focused investor. However, sometimes these higher yields can be a hidden danger. Of course, these can also turn out to be great investments if the company successfully turns things around.

Some call this value hunting. And buying beaten-down, out-of-favor stocks can lead to outperformance going forward. But in the case of the BDC sector (BIZD) and those like Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD), I would caution investors not to fall victim to their attractive yield and large discount, as I don't see it as a bargain.

In this article, I discuss Goldman Sachs BDC's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why the cheap stock price could be a hidden danger.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Goldman Sachs BDC after they reported their Q1 earnings back in July. The stock was trading at a sizable discount to its net asset value and appeared attractive.

However, disappointing earnings, deteriorating fundamentals, and economic headwinds warranted caution. During their Q1, GSBD saw their top and bottom lines decline from the prior quarter. Nll fell $0.05 to $0.42, while total investment income declined $6.9 million to $96.9 million.

The BDC saw $18 million in unrealized losses but did manage to see a slight improvement in non-performing loans. Since then, the share price has continued to decline, down close to 21%. The S&P (SP500) is up 5.5% over the same period.

Another Disappointing Quarter

Goldman Sachs BDC reported their Q3 earnings earlier this month, which I thought was another disappointing quarter despite seeing increased investment activity.

In the past quarter, several BDCs, particularly Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), have seen higher transactions. BXSL actually reported its highest activity for 2025 in their Q3, a signal that headwinds in the sector could improve going forward.

And this could result in growth in their top and bottom lines. However, GSBD's financials continued to deteriorate, with net investment income and total investment income both declining from the prior year.

Both did see slight growth from Q2 due to the aforementioned higher investment commitments and funding. Nll amounted to $0.40, up from $0.38, but down from $0.42 in Q1. Year-over-year, this was a sizable 29.8% decline from $0.57.

Lower base rates were a contributing factor. But dividend income also declined year-over-year from $469 million to $225 million. Although BDCs can see their bottom lines decline on a per-share basis, I typically look for growth on a total basis.

GSBD's net investment income also declined 33%, from $67.2 million to $44.8 million. For comparison, peer Capital Southwest (CSWC), despite Nll declining on a per-share basis from $0.64 to $0.61, actually increased from $31.165 million to $31.984 million.

As previously mentioned, they did see higher commitments and fundings during the quarter. GSBD made $470.6 million in investment commitments, with roughly $267 million funded across 27 companies.

This was up from the previous two quarters that saw $247.9 million and $87.8 million, respectively. Year-over-year these also grew from $376.6 million. Thirteen of these were new additions to the portfolio, bringing their borrower count to 171, up from 162 in Q2.

Continuous NAV Decline

One way to assess a BDC's portfolio health is by their NAV. While BDCs can see temporary declines in NAV due to declared dividends, debt repayments, etc., investors should prioritize those who see growth over time.

Ares Capital and Hercules Capital both saw NAV growth year-over-year. ARCC's grew 1.21% to $20.01, while HTGC saw higher growth of 5.7% to $12.05.

GSBD's fell 5.8% in comparison. This was due to the $0.16 special declared in Q2 as well as markdowns on underperforming borrowers. As a result, BDCs that see higher NAV growth typically perform better in terms of price appreciation.

Although we've seen 4 rate cuts since 2023, GSBD's performance vs ARCC & HTGC since 2022 tells the story of the importance of NAV growth.

Portfolio Quality

Despite adding one borrower to non-accrual status during the quarter, GSBD actually saw their non-accruals decline. These stood at 1.5% and 2.5% on a fair value and cost basis.

On a fair value basis, these declined from 1.6% in the previous quarter. And from the prior year, from 2.2% and 4.5%, respectively.

While these are manageable, further economic weakening could see these rise, resulting in tighter dividend coverage. From a credit quality standpoint, GSBD's portfolio appeared solid, with 93.4% of borrowers risk-rated two.

Dividend & Buybacks

Goldman Sachs BDC declared their $0.32 base dividend plus an additional $0.04 in supplementals expected to be paid in Q4.

And while the BDC has been generous with specials and supplementals, this is due to their earlier dividend reduction and spillover income. And by Q1'26, I suspect management will eliminate these.

Moreover, with rate cuts likely to continue, this could put further pressure on dividend coverage despite base coverage of 125%. If so, this could force management to again reduce the dividend. Although, I do think the base dividend of $0.32 is safe for the near term.

On a positive note, management is seemingly taking advantage of the steep discount to NAV and implemented a buyback program this past summer. During the quarter, they repurchased 2,136,943 shares for $25.1 million.

With a current P/NAV ratio of 0.76x and a discount close to their 5-year average, I do think buybacks are a smart use of capital. While GSBD's high starting yield and valuation may appear attractive, this is a result of deteriorating financials and an overall poor performance in the past year.

Risks

Despite seeing higher investment activity, Goldman Sachs BDC has been a poor performer. And with the recent uptick in job losses, the economy appears to be weakening further.

Going forward, investors should keep an eye on rises in non-accruals. Furthermore, many are still expecting another 25 basis point cut by January. So, for investors attracted to the high yield and large discount, more downside may be near.

Bottom Line

Goldman Sachs BDC's performance is a direct reflection of their deteriorating financials. Despite seeing higher investment activity, the declines in their top and bottom lines as well as NAV should be heeded as a warning.

As a result, the market is currently pricing this in, as evidenced by the huge discount to NAV. While their high starting yield and discount are attractive, investors should wait to see if higher investment activity will continue going forward.

If so, this could result in some bottom-line growth and allow management to avoid cutting the dividend again. While I do think their base dividend is safe for the near term, projected rate cuts and macro uncertainty keep me at a hold despite the huge discount and high yield.

data.title: 8%+ Yields: Why Plains All American Is Outperforming Energy Transfer Where It Matters Most
data.description:
In my most recent coverage of both Plains All-American Pipeline (PAA) and Energy Transfer (ET), I issued bullish takes on both, and as such, I own both in my diversified, increasingly midstream infrastructure-focused portfolio. In fact, I recently discussed why midstream infrastructure (AMLP) is my highest conviction bet at the moment in this article here.

However, in today's article, I'm going to compare PAA and ET head-to-head and share why I own much more PAA than ET today and believe it is overall a better risk-adjusted investment opportunity right now.

Why I Bought ET

When I first invested in ET, my bullish thesis was based on the fact that it has a large and well-diversified asset footprint across all major commodity basins, or across all major commodity categories, including natural gas, NGLs, crude, and refined products.

Additionally, its assets generally benefit from long-duration contracts and give it stable cash flows with limited commodity price exposure. Its natural gas business and NGL export business also give it considerable growth opportunities due to the ongoing data center build-out, in which ET should win some of those contracts, as well as the NGL export boom and the general reindustrialization effort of the United States as part of the deglobalization trend, which should further boost demand for its energy infrastructure.

Meanwhile, its huge footprint gives it numerous opportunities to invest in organic growth opportunities. Last but not least, it has an attractive distribution yield of over 8% on a next 12-month basis that is very well covered by distributable cash flow, a strong balance sheet, and management guidance for growing its distribution at a 3% to 5% CAGR, which should help it to not only keep up with but likely exceed inflation moving forward.

Why I Bought PAA

My initial investment thesis in PAA was based on the fact that it is becoming an increasingly pure-play oil midstream infrastructure business that's focused on the Permian Basin, and, between its competitively positioned and highly focused business model on one of the world's leading basins, its strong balance sheet after its multi-year focus on bringing down its leverage ratio, and its focus on returning significant capital to unit holders, PAA has turned itself into a lean-mean cash-flowing machine. In fact, its next 12-month yield is over 10%, and its valuation is one of the most attractive in the midstream sector, while it is also expected to grow its distribution at a solid clip for years to come.

Recent Results: ET and PAA Show Strength

In ET's most recent quarter, it achieved numerous new records in NGL exports, which saw 13% year-over-year growth; NGL transportation, which saw 11% year-over-year growth; refined products terminal volumes, which saw 10% year-over-year growth; and interstate gas transportation, which saw 8% year-over-year growth. The company has also brought on substantial long-term firm capacity contracts over the past year with a weighted average life of 18 years and more than $25 billion in future fees. This shows the vast capacity that ET brings to bear and its highly visible growth pipeline.

Meanwhile, PAA continued to execute very well, with tariff volume increases and bolt-on acquisitions combining to deliver a strong $593 million adjusted EBITDA performance from its crude segment, while its EPIC acquisition, which has now become fully consolidated due to acquiring the remaining economic interest in the pipeline, gives them significant opportunities to accelerate and increase synergy capture from it and invest in adjacent organic growth opportunities.

Where ET Falls Short

While there is a lot to like about both companies, what sets PAA apart from ET is the fact that ET is generating remarkably weak growth rates relative to the amount that it is investing in growth projects. ET continues to add massive investment projects like its recently announced $5.3 billion Desert Southwest Pipeline project and its long-lead-time natural gas projects that run into 2027 to 2029. While all this spending signals that it should be able to generate strong growth for investors, ET's actual bottom-line growth has been pretty unimpressive in recent years relative to its massive capital spending. This is showing up in its relatively flat distributable cash flow per unit growth since 2022.

Moreover, the distribution growth has been fairly weak in the last two years as well, coming in at just a little bit over 3% each year.

What this shows is that while ET is investing a lot, and perhaps even these growth opportunities are genuinely exciting, it is ultimately generating weak returns on capital on these projects, and/or its existing legacy businesses are effectively melting ice cubes that are shrinking on an organic basis, thereby consuming much of the growth that this heavy spending is bringing forth for them. This makes ET's valuation multiple much less compelling than it appears on the surface and helps to explain why it looks so cheap compared to peers:

Where PAA Outperforms

In contrast, PAA is not spending nearly as aggressively as ET is on growth. Yet, despite selling off core assets in recent years and focusing on deleveraging rather than spending big on capital projects, PAA has managed to grow its distributable cash flow per unit at a 4.8% CAGR over that same time period while also growing its distribution per unit at a 19.3% CAGR.

This indicates that PAA's organic performance is much better than ET's and that they, in general, have been more accretive allocators of capital than ET has been.

Moreover, with their growing focus on their circle of competence, namely oil midstream infrastructure in the Permian Basin, the chances of them misallocating capital and inefficiently handling assets are further declining. Therefore, when you combine PAA's better organic performance with its more attractive yield of 10.3% on a 12-month basis compared to ET's 8.1% yield on a next 12-month basis, as well as its expected distribution per unit CAGR of 5.9% compared to ET's distribution per unit 5.2% CAGR over the same period, PAA's yield plus growth profile looks significantly more attractive than ET's.

Focused vs. Aggressive CAPEX

Additionally, there is a lot more risk to ET's model because they are pursuing a more aggressive CAPEX program that pushes free cash flow well into the future, whereas PAA is pursuing a much more focused CAPEX investment program and is instead returning capital more aggressively to investors in the near term.

The main argument in favor of ET, though, remains that it is much better diversified and that its exposure to natural gas and NGLs gives it a better long-term growth profile. While this is certainly true, if I could only hold one MLP in my entire portfolio and I had to pick between PAA or ET, I would pick ET simply due to its broad diversification and greater scale.

However, if I am simply looking for one stock to add to my diversified portfolio that I think offers the best risk-adjusted total return potential for the next five years, I would pick PAA because it has proven to be a much better organic performer as it does not need to reinvest nearly as much distributable cash flow to generate similar or even stronger growth rates than ET has while also paying out a significantly higher distribution. Additionally, it is expected to grow its distribution at a slightly faster clip than ET is moving forward. Finally, its balance sheet is less leveraged than ET's is, as ET's leverage is around four times, and PAA's is only at 3.3 times. This gives it significant flexibility, if needed, to add additional leverage to make opportunistic investments or even repurchase preferred or common units.

Risk Analysis and Investor Takeaway

While the market may be concerned about PAA's concentration in oil, numerous forecasters expect resilient oil demand for decades to come as population growth and rising living standards in developing economies support global oil demand, OPEC+'s spare capacity is shrinking, there has been limited significant investment in mega oil production projects across the world, which puts somewhat of an upper limit on supply increases, and there is growing long-term reliance on U.S. shale production. Last, but not least, the Permian Basin is positioned to remain one of the world's premier oil production basins for years to come.

Therefore, while I like ET and continue to rate it a buy, I like PAA even more right now and currently rate it a strong buy, while recently making it one of the largest positions in my portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia Q3: There's A Red Flag In The Report, But I'm Still Buying
data.description:
It's that time again, when the whole market pays attention to the earnings of one company for a moment, and headlines go out about whether Nvidia Corp (NVDA) will make or break the market with its results.

No wonder, when a single line in an earnings result can move the entire market, due to the sheer amount of concentration of NVDA stock in popular indexes like the S&P 500 (which is 7% NVDA). Its reach is enormous, and even if you don't own any shares of Nvidia, its performance impacts your investments.

Good news for all involved parties, they beat expectations, and their stock was rewarded. Will that cure the market's latest stumble? Unclear yet. But it at least bucked the trend for a day.

NVDA Q3 Earnings Overview

Let's get straight into it. NVDA beat on top line and bottom, and continues to justify the buy rating I gave it back in August. There are few companies that I believe are widow-makers for short sellers; NVDA is one of them.

Here's the most-watched figures, all beats.

Estimates Actual
Gross Revenue $55.19B $57.01B
Data Center Rev. $49.34B $51.20B
Earnings per share $1.26 $1.30

So far, so good. That looks like a stellar print, and it is.

Of course, there was much focus on the data center figure, which came in not only above expectations, but also above trend. This is a good sign for the company, which has a valuation that requires perfection in execution. These are the kinds of results investors are looking for.

Gaming has its own cycle, and is not nearly as impactful to revenue as data center usage. It came at lower than last quarter, and is mostly dismissible for investors, as it's a market that does not have nearly the same growth prospects as compute.

Believe it or not, that wasn't the sore spot of this earnings call for me. It was the margins. It wasn't the "other" category, which came in above-trend for professional use, and at-trend for automotive and embedded sales.

Gross Margins Lagging, Could Be Blackwell

Don't listen to the Bloomberg headline in this chart; it's written positively, but holding is not what we need to see from Nvidia here from where I'm sitting. I consider this a mark against them, but it hasn't shaken my confidence in my buy rating.

Regular readers will remember that I feel comfortable giving out buy ratings at this stage in the market cycle because I'm a believer in profit-adjusted valuations driving the AI boom; you can read more about it from an article I wrote last week, "Are We Valuing the Markets Incorrectly?"

The short of it is that on a profitability-adjusted basis, the current market is not nearly as overvalued as traditional indicators show. We have a good reason to believe that a higher profitability allows for a higher sustained multiple expansion, since the market has been acting this way for the last 25 years.

Watch the drift on the chart not only from bottom to top (higher valuation multiples), but also left to right (higher profitability). The correlation is high.

So long as margins to continue to rise, valuations can be sustained while we wait for earnings to materialize.

Here's the key chart from that article:

With Nvidia as the clear leader in the market, and a large part of the growth story in AI, its profitability plays a big factor in this whole calculation, and a drop in this figure could be a signal that markets are actually overvalued.

The good news on this front is that the current production cycle for Blackwell, Nvidia's latest chip, is underway and in its most capital-intensive stages. This means that we may see a rise in profitability as they move into later stages of production and become more efficient.

This is a key figure to watch moving forward, and one that was held up for now, and potentially justified. However, any deterioration of this figure could be met with a swift selloff from the market, so investors beware.

My Signal To Exit Nvidia: Dark Compute

As we ran up to this earnings call, I had a friend pose a question to me, "How will we know when the bubble is going to pop?"

Of course, this is standard fare investor blabber, but I thought the answer would be insightful for some readers, as it concerns Nvidia directly.

One of the most glaring symptoms of an infrastructure bubble is when infrastructure is being put in place without existing demand ready to absorb it. The easiest example to compare this to is the Dot Com Bubble, where companies were laying "dark fiber," or fiber optic cables not being put to use. They were doing this on the expectation of future demand growth, which didn't and couldn't materialize in the timeline needed to secure investor returns.

I've been on record saying that one of the key differences, and a reason to continue owning this market, is that there are no dark GPUs. All of NVDA's chips sold are being immediately put to use, and as CEO Jensen Huang said on the earnings call, they are "sold out."

Currently, the limiting factor for NVDA's growth is their supply chain. They just cannot produce enough GPUs to fill demand.

Rethinking my thesis in an answer to my friend, I said that I think GPUs will never be the limiting factor in the AI arms race taking place. It will be electricity, and the ability to power data centers. So I'm amending my statement about "there are no dark GPUs."

I think there will likely never be dark GPUs, at least not like there was dark fiber. Instead, the GPUs will be installed and Nvidia will profit and move on, but the data center may not be powered on, due to an insufficient power grid. This is one of the limiting factors of the current AI build out, and is likely to get worse over time unless serious changes take place in America's willingness and ability to build power infrastructure.

In this way, we will not see dark compute building up on Nvidia's books. They will likely look clean, even if we do see this bubble-symptom emerge.

If we do see dark compute, we will see it in Nvidia's partners first, pure hyperscalers like Nebius NV (NBIS) and Coreweave (CRWV). That would be a signal to me that we are clearly in "get out" territory, as that would forecast a fall in Nvidia's future sales that could act like a black swan to those who just follow Nvidia. It will likely not show up on their books until it's too late, as there will be no dark GPUs, but we could see the emergence of dark compute.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this was a great report from Nvidia. Beating on the top and bottom is something that many investors have come to expect from the behemoth.

I can be counted in as an investor who expected them to come out swinging, and I was impressed with everything but their gross margins, which were stagnant (Bloomberg called them "holding strong", two sides of every story, I suppose). However, this lapse in profitability could be due to Nvidia's rollout of its latest Blackwell chips, so time will tell if this is a real issue or not.

It has not shaken my confidence in my buy rating.

What would shake my confidence is the emergence of dark compute, or data centers being completed without access to power, and promises from operating firms to build out the power infrastructure at a later date. This mirrors a bubble symptom from the Dot Com era, and could be a real threat to the growth of Nvidia, but may not show up on Nvidia's earnings right away, since the data centers would still complete purchases of GPUs.

I am still watching for this, but for now, I do not see any evidence that investors should abandon their Nvidia positions. I maintain my buy rating.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Hilton Grand Vacations: Technical Risks And Insider Selling Still Outweigh Everything
data.description:
Since my initial coverage of Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc. (HGV), its value has already decreased by 42% after reaching its 12-month high of $51 and justified my bullish rating before. Now, it experiences market softness amid macroeconomic uncertainty that continues to squeeze travel budget. Even so, it remains resilient with its strong brand recognition, sales conversion, and client/member retention. And while its debt level warrants caution, HGV is still well-positioned to repay it on time while sustaining its operating capacity. However, technicals present bearish signals as momentum weakens further on top of the recent insider selling.

HGV Q3 2025: Challenged But Cautiously Positioned

Amid stubborn inflation and macroeconomic uncertainty, discretionary spending remains soft. Even tourism sees the same trend as 2025 marks the end of revenge travel. Yet, luxury properties and travel services remain quite unfazed as their demand remains resilient. One perfect example is Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc.

In Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.300B, which was relatively flat from the same quarter in 2024 at $1.306B. Its weakness was evident in the sales of VOIs, which decreased by 14% YoY. This means that the value of all the timeshares sold during the quarter decreased. We can prove it if we look at its revenue per segment because real estate sales and financing decreased YoY. Despite this, I believe that this was mainly due to its pricing strategies and business model diversification to maintain demand. After all, its contract sales rose by 17% to $907M versus $777M YoY. In addition, all the remaining revenue components remained strong, which shows that HGV was able to dictate higher rates or fees for its services, resort and club management, and commissions.

Moreover, it was able to manage costs and expenses amid rising prices. One aspect to consider is the decrease in its cost of VOI sales. Its percentage decreased from 13.6% to 9.1% YoY, which means that it has become more efficient in its actual timeshare selling operations. Other expenses increased, but these were primarily related to SG&A expenses. With that, it's not surprising that HGV remained profitable with net income margin remaining flat at 2% YoY.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation around the world remains a challenge for travel-related stocks. Today, inflation has already picked up momentum and returned to its 14-month high of 3.0%. New tariffs may intensify it due to their inflationary nature. That is why discretionary spending may still be affected this quarter. In fact, a study a few months ago revealed that consumers might lower their holiday spending, and this may include travel.

I’m also concerned about its high borrowings, which are already about $7.3B. The amount has also nearly doubled in the past 11 quarters. Now, its Net Debt/EBITDA is already 8.4x, which means that HGV may need about eight years to repay all borrowings at once. The consolation is that its borrowings have different maturities. It can also refinance its borrowings to ensure liquidity.

Why HGV Stays Strategic

Despite these potential risks, I believe that HGV can insulate its operations.

One aspect to consider is its hybrid business model. HGV mixes its traditional timeshare component with a point-based club model. This offers more flexibility in how its members utilize their ownership. This is like combining an ownership/lease model with a fee-based model. HGV members have a deeded interest in a specific property, so they can use it to their heart’s content for a definite period or week. For its points-based system, which functions like a fee-based model, members can use their points to book at different HGV-owned resorts or partners instead of being tied to a single location. But they have to pay ongoing maintenance fees. Considering these perks, it’s not surprising that it enjoys a solid customer base. In 2024, it already had a total of 724,000 members, 59% higher than in 2019. Although it decreased to 721,488 last Q3, HGV’s strong domestic and international presence and strategic business model can help protect its market position.

Relative to its peers, Hilton remains competitive with 235M loyalty members. This was already an increase of 17M from 218M in my previous coverage. Marriott Bonvoy (VAC) maintains its lead, but HGV remains highly competitive. These two brands are still far ahead of Wyndham Rewards (WH) and World Of Hyatt (WH).

Another potential insulation or risk mitigant of HGV is its actual business. Timeshare is a luxury good or service, which is contrary to hotels and apartments, which appear more inclusive. Timeshares target individuals or families from upper-income levels, especially high-net-worth individuals or HNWIs. These people are typically inflation-resistant since their earnings are still high relative to prices. So, demand is more resilient in these groups.

What’s more interesting is that the ultra-rich people appear to be giving up on luxury assets but shifting to luxury services. Considering the nature of timeshares, which are rights of use, I believe this falls under luxury services. After all, it offers travel experience, not products. This is consistent with the increasing popularity of luxury travel. I think this behavioral shift can be traced back to the central bank’s efforts to increase liquidity after the Global Financial Crisis. The Quantitative Easing or QE was able to hand a lot of money to the wealthiest. Over the years, inflation (it typically includes normal goods like bread, wheat, milk, and eggs) has stabilized. But because of increased liquidity, luxury assets became more attractive. But after the series of interest rate hikes, QE is nowhere to be found. The interest rate in the US is still elevated, which makes bonds and structured products/investments more attractive than luxury boats, mansions, wines, and watches.

Valuation

As I visited its valuation again, I realized that my price ratios were not foolproof because I included 2020. As a travel-related stock, the pandemic eroded its value and financials due to heavy lockdowns with zero tolerance for the most part of the year. To be more precise, I started in 2021. Using P/B, the stock now trades at 2.26x versus the four-year average of 2.39x. This shows that HGV is still reasonable today. If we use the average to derive the fair value, the TP or target price will be $40.55. If we add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will become $38.53. Either way, HGV still trades at a logical level. However, it appears that it’s still fully valued today, so upside may remain limited.

Technicals: Insider Selling And Negative Sentiments

HGV’s technicals adhere to valuation as bearish signals persist. The stock price keeps decreasing with lower highs and lows below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that its downtrend still holds. It also means that the price trend today remains weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has already shifted downward and is about to cross below the 200-day SMA. This suggests that market sentiments are already pessimistic. I cannot blame the market because of the insider selling in the past three months. Earlier this month, two officers sold over 30,000 shares in only a week.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the trend. The MACD line moves below the signal line, which means that selling volume keeps increasing. It is also within negative territory along with the histogram, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. We can only note that the histogram is approaching the neutral zone again. As we can see, there have been buying attempts as the price breaks multiple support levels. Also, RSI shows that the stock is nearly oversold, so some investors may be taking this opportunity to buy the stock at a discount.

Conclusion

Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc. remains well-positioned despite macroeconomic uncertainty and high borrowings. Its strategic business model and resilient demand of high-income individuals can support its solid customer base. Valuation is still reasonable, although it’s almost fully valued. However, technical risks due to bearish signals and recent insider selling may continue to affect market sentiments. After weighing these aspects, I decided to downgrade it from buy to hold for now.

data.title: Chime Financial: We're Doubling Down On This Fast Growing Neobank
data.description:
In June of this year, we wrote an article titled "3 Reasons Chime's IPO Looks Like A Strong Buy".

In that article, we made the case that shares of Chime Financial (CHYM) - the fast growing, fee free, high margin Neobank - looked like a great long-term opportunity.

While the bank was (and is) relatively small in in the grand scheme of things, it's growing members quickly, and the business model is dead simple: acquire and monetize customers through a handful of checking account and credit products.

In recent years, the company has managed to grow users rapidly, and the bank's unit economics appear incredibly strong, with an LTV/CAC of higher than 8x(!).

However, since the IPO, the stock has cratered by roughly half, making our call in June one of the worst we’ve ever had on Seeking Alpha:

Right now, there’s negative momentum in the stock, and some investors may be squeamish about buying in. That said, we think the recent price action represents an opportunity, not a risk.

As the business keeps acquiring new users and deepening monetization efforts, we expect that shares in this Robinhood-style (HOOD) banking upstart will appreciate materially.

Today, we'll dive deep into the companies recent earnings report, argue that the valuation appears way too cheap, and explain why we’re doubling down on CHYM.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

If you haven’t heard of Chime Financial before, the underlying thesis is this; the company is an upstart Neobank, with a simple-to-use app that allows customers to save money, spend money, build credit and more.

The bank started out originally as a fee-free alternative to traditional, expensive retail banking solutions, and has since expanded to prepaid consumer credit cards, fee-free overdraft, early paycheck credit, and a number of other retail-friendly financial products.

The business model is extremely simple: when consumers direct deposit their paycheck into CHYM, they tend to spend most (or all) of it, using the firm's credit and debit cards, which the bank then earns interchange fees on. To be clear, this revenue source does expose Chime to considerable counterparty risk (card processors). That said, at this level, consumer spending is incredibly stable through cycles, as consumers making less than $100,000 a year tend to spend, not save, their paychecks.

As a result, Chime has seen stable margins and steady growth.

Now, with the company’s recent migration to its own fully owned proprietary tech stack, we expect the business to unlock significant operating leverage in the future:

Recently, the company reported Q3 earnings, pulling in revenue of $543 million and beating analyst estimates with positive earnings per share of $0.08:

These numbers are solid, but we’re really excited about the progress CHYM made in terms unit economics.

All consumer-based businesses tend to be ruled by a single financial relationship: LTV to CAC. LTV being the amount of revenue that a customer brings in over their lifetime, and CAC being the cost of acquiring that customer.

When LTV goes up, or CAC goes down, it tends to drive expanding operating leverage and stronger profits for shareholders. This is exactly what we’re seeing with CHYM. In Q3, the company announced 21% YoY member growth, adding 1.6 million active members TTM while managing to actually reduce CAC by 10%:

This shows that CHYM has considerable word-of-mouth and scale advantages, given that CPM’s have gone up over the last 12 months. Not only that, but LTV is also growing, as the bank's average revenue per active member increased 6% YoY:

This reflects higher levels of spending and engagement, alongside the release of new products that give CHYM a wider surface area to monetize.

Combined with the overall increase in members, these figures imply tremendous scaling potential for the business:

On the profit side, the company keeps about 70% of its overall revenue as 'transaction' margin. Up until recently, the business spent this remaining 70% on operating expenses; building out tech teams, customer support, and other fixed cost centers.

Now, however, we believe the business is set to rapidly expand EBITDA, as these fixed cost centers will not need to grow linearly with members. Instead, the investments CHYM has made in years' past will likely begin to bear fruit, which is something we’re already seeing.

In Q3, Chime produced $29 million in adjusted EBITDA with a 5% overall margin:

We expect this margin to expand to 15% plus over the next year with further room for scaling thereafter:

When viewed in sum, CHYM is a growing Neobank with a simple business model, improving margins, and a rapidly growing customer base.

As the business continues to add customers and release new products, we're expecting rapid margin expansion that could go to the double digits in just a few short quarters.

This mirrors, Robinhood’s growth trajectory in the brokerage space, as it brought fees down across the industry, built up a new active trading user base, and deepened monetization with Robinhood Gold and other products.

We believe CHYM is on a similar path.

Valuation

But how much does the stock cost?

When we wrote about the stock in June, shares were trading it roughly 5x blended sales.

Now, with the stock dropping roughly 50%, the price/sales ratio has also fallen to under 2x. This number has fallen even more than 50% as a result of the firm's underlying revenue growth, which has been strong.

For our money, we see CHYM in the same way that we see other strong new age banking companies like SoFi Technologies (SOFI) and Nu Financial (NU).

While those companies are further along their growth curve and have deeper monetization already built out, we expect CHYM to follow a similar path. Not only that, but CHYM' growth rate appears quite similar to these other banks.

And yet, NU and SOFI trade at anywhere from 9x to 11x sales:

Robinhood, an even larger, more scaled competitor (that has recently dipped its toe into retail banking) trades at an even richer 25x sales.

Chime, however, trades at 1.4x trailing sales, even though the company has a similar business model and unit economics, etc.

Again, the net profits aren't there, and in some cases, the growth isn't quite as impressive (SOFI grew revenues 37% YoY).

Overall, though, this doesn’t make a lot of sense to us, which is the reason why we’re doubling down on the stock.

To be clear, this isn’t something we typically do - especially if a stock has performed as poorly as CHYM has - but nothing fundamental has changed with the company. The products are widely loved, users are growing rapidly, and the economics are good. The only conclusion we can come to is that the TTM profitability inflection has not yet occurred in a meaningful way, which is why the market has not yet taken an interest.

We believe this is myopic, and an oversight that will likely be corrected in the coming year.

As a result, we're reiterating our Strong Buy rating on Chime Financial.

Risks

As far as risks go, we see two core areas that investors should keep an eye on.

First off, as was apparent from the valuation comparison above, CHYM is a simple business, but it’s not an entirely unique business. There are several other competitors in the space, including SoFi, Robinhood, Current and more.

If Chime fails to win new business, then the growth story dries up, and the margins don’t make a whole lot of sense.

Secondly, as we mentioned earlier, CHYM is - to some degree - at the mercy of credit card processing companies like Visa (V) and MasterCard (MA), given that such a large percentage of the company’s business comes from interchange fees.

If anything were to change in CHYM's relationship with current providers, then there's a serious risk to the whole business.

That said, there’s considerable economic incentives for both Chime and Visa to remain constructive in their partnership. As a result, we think this is a concentration risk, but not a material business risk.

Summary

When viewed from the top down, Chime is a robust, growing bank with a straightforward business model and significant operating leverage. Trading at an 80% discount to other stocks with similar business models, we expect that as margins improve, CHYM should see it’s multiple reinflate, thus driving considerable gains for shareholders.

As a result, we’re doubling down, and reiterating our Strong Buy rating.

Cheers~

data.title: SHYD: Weak Muni Bond ETF, Better Choices Out There
data.description:
In keeping with my continued coverage on bond funds, thought to have another look at the VanEck Short High Yield Muni ETF (BATS: SHYD). SHYD offers investors a tax-advantaged 3.5% dividend yield, with few other important benefits. It is a comparatively low yield, on both an absolute and relative basis. I find the fund's overall value proposition and current yield to be uncompelling, so I would not be investing in the fund at the present time.

SHYD - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

SHYD is a simple, diversified short-term high yield municipal bond index ETF. Although I was unable to find detailed information about its underlying index, this overview, and the description by VanEck, both seem straightforward enough.

SHYD's portfolio is quite diversified within its niche, with investments in almost 500 different securities, and with exposure to several states and most sectors.

SHYD is quite diversified for a muni bond ETF. It lacks the diversification of broader ETFs investing in several bond sub-asset classes, including the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ: BND) and the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF 9 (NYSEARCA: AGG).

For reference, the VanEck High Yield Muni ETF (HYD) is a bit more diversified than SHYD, with investments in 1,766 securities, and with exposure to longer-term muni bonds.

Credit Risk

SHYD focuses on high-yield non-investment grade bonds, but with a sizable contingent of investment-grade bonds. Credit ratings are as follows, most not rated bonds are of lower quality.

Although SHYD's credit ratings are below-average, actual credit quality is high, as municipal issuers rarely default. Non-investment grade muni bonds have a 10y cumulative default rate of only 6.84%, equivalent to just 0.684% per year. For reference, non-investment grade corporate bond default rates reach 29.81% at the 10y mark, more than 4x those of muni bonds.

Due to the above, the fund should see small, below-average losses during downturns and recessions. This has not been the case in the past, with SHYD seeing broadly comparable losses to benchmark high-yield corporate bond ETFs during early 2020, the most recent downturn.

In my opinion, the above was due to a combination of perceptions of risk and wider credit spreads. Non-investment grade muni bonds have weak credit ratings and so saw losses during the pandemic, even though default rates themselves remained low. Due to the sharpness of the pandemic, selling of municipal bonds did make some sense, at the time at least.

In my opinion, SHYD's strong credit quality is a long-term benefit for investors, even though the impact has been quite muted during prior downturns.

SHYD and HYD have broadly comparable credit quality, HYD a bit weaker.

HYD saw slightly higher losses during early 2020, in-line with the above.

SHYD's slightly higher credit quality is a small advantage relative to HYD, but an advantage nonetheless.

Interest Rate Risk

SHYD focuses on short-term municipal bonds, and so the fund has a lower average maturity and duration than its peers, including HYD.

Due to the above, the fund should outperform when rates rise, as was the case from 2022 to 2024, during which Federal Reserve rates sky-rocketed. SHYD did underperform high-yield bonds, as these securities have even lower durations.

Flipside of the above is that SHYD should underperform when rates decrease. As is the case for most bond ETFs, prices and performance are dependent on market rates of interest, which are influenced by Federal Reserve rates, but not directly set by the Fed. At the same time, prices are influenced by other factors, including investor sentiment and credit spreads. Significant cuts should result in underperformance, more minor cuts might not. SHYD has underperformed YTD:

But did not underperform during late 2024:

Point being, SHYD has underperformed during the more recent set of cuts, didn't underperform during the previous one. Further cuts might result in further underperformance, but they very well might not.

Dividend Analysis

SHYD currently sports a 3.5% dividend yield, very low on an absolute basis, and lower than that of most bonds and bond sub-asset classes.

SHYD's dividends do have one advantage: their tax-exempt status. Most dividends are taxable at the federal level, SHYD's dividends are not. This is a straightforward benefit for investors, and one which might be particularly impactful for investors in higher tax brackets.

SHYD provides the following table of taxable equivalent yields for investors in different tax brackets:

As an example of what these figures mean, for investors with a federal tax rate of 37%, SHYD's yield is equivalent to a 5.9% taxable yield. If a taxable fund yields less than 5.9%, it would provide less in after-tax income than SHYD. If a taxable fund yields more than 5.9%, it would provide more in after-tax income after taxes than SHYD.

SHYD's after-tax yield is competitive with that of most bonds and bond sub-asset classes, at least for investors in the higher tax brackets. High-yield corporate bonds do yield more, at significantly higher credit risk, moderately weaker credit ratings.

SHYD's dividend yield compares unfavorably to some of its tax-advantaged peers though.

Specifically, the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) offers investors a (potentially) tax-advantaged 4.6% yield (to option expiration).

With the stability of t-bills:

BOXX's returns should closely track t-bill rates, so that 4.6% yield should decline in the coming months / weeks. Still, current Fed policy is such that BOXX should outearn SHYD, and with significantly lower volatility. Seems like a superior investment to me, although more dovish investors might disagree.

HYD provides investors with a tax-advantaged 4.4% SEC yield, compared to SHYD's 3.7% SEC yield. HYD is somewhat riskier, more volatile, than SHYD, but not significantly so. HYD's dividends should decline as the Fed cuts rates, but much slower than SHYD.

In my opinion, HYD is on net a stronger, higher-yielding investment than SHYD.

Overall, SHYD's dividends are simply too low relative to peers, even after accounting for any potential tax benefits.

Performance Track-Record

As a final point, SHYD's performance track-record seems fine, with higher returns than most bonds and treasuries, lower than investment-grade and high-yield corporate bonds, as well as HYD.

In my opinion, SHYD's performance track-record is not strong enough to outweigh its weak dividends, specially considering the fact that HYD has significantly outperformed the fund.

Conclusion

SHYD focuses on short-term, high-yield muni bonds. SCHD offers investors a tax-advantaged 3.7% SEC yield, an overall low yield even after accounting for any potential tax benefits. As such, I would not be investing in the fund at the present time.

data.title: Microsoft Brings Agents To Office And Windows
data.description:
While most companies are still struggling with how to best integrate AI into their organizations, it’s clear that the businesses building the tools for these companies have quickly moved on to agents as the primary means of leveraging the capabilities of artificial intelligence. This was abundantly clear at the keynote for Microsoft’s (MSFT) Ignite event in San Francisco, where the Redmond-based giant unveiled numerous ways to create, integrate, and manage AI-powered agents into its Microsoft 365 suite of productivity apps as well as Windows 11.

Most of the reason for the focus on agents is straightforward - it’s about turning intelligence into action. By leveraging the reasoning capabilities of today’s most advanced AI models in conjunction with the context of a user’s files, emails, calendar events, chats, etc., the thought is that you can start performing useful work on behalf of an individual. It’s an intoxicating idea that provides a great example of the potential power of applied AI - as long as the reality can live up to the hype (which, in some early efforts, hasn’t always been the case).

A critical part of making this vision work is having a solid foundation of context onto which potential applications can be applied. At Ignite, Microsoft introduced a new contextual layer of intelligence it’s calling Work IQ that provides that context. While not immediately clear, it turns out Work IQ builds on top of the data in the existing Microsoft Graph. In other words, as a major LLM succinctly explained, “Work IQ essentially takes the ‘what’ from Graph and transforms it into the ‘why’ and ‘how’ for organizations.”

With the Work IQ layer in place, the options for integrating agents into the experience are manifold. To start, it’s possible to leverage prebuilt agents either in the Copilot chat experience or directly in Word, Excel, and now PowerPoint via new Agent modes. So, depending on which way you prefer to work, you can get access to tools that can help you create Office documents with simple prompts and then the necessary follow-up questions to get files with the content and format you want. It’s great to see Microsoft offer this degree of flexibility, but it can be a bit confusing for people who are still trying to figure out how to integrate these AI-powered tools into their everyday workflows.

If you want to create custom agents, Microsoft offers several ways to do that, many of which were updated at Build. Copilot Studio continues to be the tool of choice for power users and “citizen developers” to create their own agents, but the company also discussed Microsoft Foundry (the new name for Azure Foundry) for professional developers, as well as a new Agent 365 SDK (Software Development Kit). Important additions for these latter tools include the ability to run these agents in their own separate environments - not just containers - giving IT managers more opportunity to manage and control them - a point to be discussed more a bit later.

Agents built with Microsoft Foundry can now (at least in preview) also maintain a memory across sessions. This provides critical historical context as people use the tools for multiple tasks. Plus, it’s a capability that most people have grown accustomed to with the big LLM chatbots. Microsoft also made a big announcement with Anthropic (and Nvidia (NVDA)) about the companies working together. One of the direct outcomes of this collaboration is that Microsoft now includes support for Anthropic’s Claude, Haiku, and other models as an option when building agents. This provides yet more flexibility for developers to try out different underlying AI models as they build out their agents.

Building on the concept of Work IQ, Microsoft also announced Foundry IQ, which essentially extends the conceptual connections that Work IQ brings to individuals across the entire business. This allows agents built with Foundry to leverage all the available enterprise documents and resources in places like SharePoint directories via a RAG-style implementation. This, in turn, makes the agents more grounded in company data (and therefore, less likely to hallucinate).

In addition to the traditional Office apps of Microsoft 365, the company also introduced several new agentic capabilities within Windows 11. Windows has had the ability to do a semantic search and to change certain Windows settings via the Copilot app for a few months now. At Build, the company announced a new core set of services and capabilities within Windows 11 - including an integrated MCP server and what the company calls an Agent Framework - that turn it into what the company initially dubbed an “agentic OS.” While that description isn’t likely to stick, it does highlight how the company is working to make Windows 11 a more friendly environment in which to create and run agents.

Leveraging Work IQ, the company showed how the latest version of Copilot can look across emails, calendar invites, chats, et al as it responds to questions users may have about finding information, planning for a meeting, etc. Not surprisingly, Microsoft was quick to point out that IT shops and users can choose which set of agentic capabilities they want to run (or block) in Windows, but the foundation for agents in Windows is now being put into place.

Microsoft also brought a new means to run certain types of agents in virtual desktop environments via Windows 365. Windows agents can leverage the company’s cloud-based Windows 365 virtual machines to create separate environments in which agents perform their tasks. This is conceptually similar to what Foundry brings to agents that run across an entire enterprise and can help with both manageability and security of those agents. Another potentially very useful capability coming soon to Windows is the ability to use the Edge browser with an agent to do things like fill out online forms and other computer usage (CUA - Computer Usage Agent) applications.

Of course, given all these tools for building and deploying agents, it’s not hard to imagine scenarios in which agents could potentially run amok. To help avoid this, the company debuted a new tool called Microsoft Agent 365, which it described as a “control plane for agents.” While it may sound like a relatively straightforward extension to the company’s other agent-focused offerings, it’s interesting on several levels. First, it essentially treats agents as “digital employees” complete with different sets of access rights, digital IDs, and more. Because of that approach, Microsoft integrated its Defender, Entra, and Purview tools as part of Agent 365. Until now, these tools have only been applied to people, but applying them to agents allows IT managers to see and manage the activities of agents just as they do regular employees. This, in turn, provides a set of governance and security controls that can help with potentially nefarious digital agents exfiltrating proprietary corporate data or performing other harmful tasks. While the anthropomorphizing of digital agents does feel a bit odd, given all the capabilities they will soon have, it only makes sense.

Microsoft also debuted a new Foundry Control Plane that’s designed for developers who are creating agents with the new Foundry tools. While the tools might initially seem to overlap, the best way to understand the distinction between them is to know that Agent 365 is how the enterprise sees and governs agents, while Foundry Control Plane is how developers see and govern agents. It’s a lot to put together, but at the same time, it does reflect how thoroughly Microsoft has thought about the requirements for building and deploying agents across a wide range of users and roles throughout the enterprise. Of course, it also shows how complicated all of this still remains.

We’ve seen many different companies discuss their agentic efforts recently to help position themselves as forward thinkers, and in some ways, Microsoft essentially did the same thing at Ignite. However, once you dig in, you can see that the range and depth of the agentic-based offerings that Microsoft discussed at the event go well beyond what virtually any other company has yet to unveil. In all honesty, that can make the overall message difficult to decipher, and I think the company still has a lot of work to do to simplify and clarify how all its tools fit together. But there’s no question that Microsoft is placing a big stake in the ground when it comes to extremely wide deployment of agents in business environments. Now, let’s just hope that businesses can keep up with the pace of these announcements.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.
